---
title: "Exhaustiveness"
---

## "I added a 7th `RowDecision` case." {.unnumbered}

This is the chapter where the ladder bites hardest. Imagine product
asks for a new outcome:

> `Throttled` — the row is fine, but the upstream API rate-limited
> us. Re-queue it for later. Do not skip, do not reject.

You add the case. What happens?

```{mermaid}
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Added: Add Throttled case
    Added --> NormalCS: normal C#
    Added --> IdiomaticCS: idiomatic C#
    Added --> Fsharp: F#
    Added --> Haskell: Haskell
    Added --> Rust: Rust

    NormalCS --> Silent: 0 errors, 0 warnings
    Silent --> Production: ships
    Production --> BugReport: misposted batches

    IdiomaticCS --> Runtime: build green
    Runtime --> RuntimeThrow: InvalidOperationException at first match
    RuntimeThrow --> Hotfix

    Fsharp --> WarningF: FS0025 non-exhaustive match
    WarningF --> WerrorF: --warnaserror -> compile error
    WerrorF --> Caught

    Haskell --> CompileH: -Wall + -Werror -> compile error
    CompileH --> Caught

    Rust --> CompileR: E0004 non-exhaustive patterns
    CompileR --> Caught
```

Five engines, five different moments at which the compiler tells you
about the missing case (or doesn't).

## Normal C# — silent

Add `public const string Throttled = "Throttled";` to your row's
`Status` field. Now go look at the consumer:

```csharp
// normalcsharp-fuel-engine/src/FuelUploadEngine/Services/FuelUploadService.cs
switch (d.Status)
{
    case "Accepted":              s.Accepted++; break;
    case "AcceptedWithWarnings":  s.AcceptedWithWarnings++; break;
    case "Quarantined":           s.Quarantined++; break;
    case "Skipped":               s.Skipped++; break;
    case "Rejected":              s.Rejected++; break;
    case "Fatal":                 s.Fatal++; break;
    // No default branch -- any "new" status string we forget
    // to handle here will silently fail to be counted.
}
```

No default branch. Throttled rows are produced (somewhere) and then
**silently dropped from the summary counts**. The totals stop adding
up. No test fails. No exception is thrown. Production keeps running.
The bug is discovered three weeks later when finance reconciles.

This is footgun #7 in [the seven footguns
chapter](../part1/03-seven-footguns.qmd).

## Idiomatic C# — runtime throw

```csharp
return decision switch
{
    RowDecision.AcceptedTransaction accepted        => /* ... */,
    RowDecision.AcceptedTransactionWithWarnings ... => /* ... */,
    RowDecision.QuarantinedRow quarantined          => /* ... */,
    RowDecision.SkippedDuplicate skipped            => /* ... */,
    RowDecision.RejectedRow rejected                => /* ... */,
    RowDecision.FatalProcessingError fatal          => /* ... */,
    _ => throw new InvalidOperationException("Unhandled row decision.")
};
```

You add `RowDecision.ThrottledRow`. The code compiles — the
`_ =>` default catches it. Tests probably pass (none of them produce
a `ThrottledRow` yet). The bug ships. First production batch that
emits a `Throttled` row crashes the response mapper at the runtime
throw.

This is **strictly better than normal C#**: the failure is loud, the
stack trace points at the unhandled case. But it's still runtime, and
the compiler had all the information it needed to refuse the build.
It just doesn't.

The reason is that C#'s exhaustiveness analysis for switch
expressions on sealed-record hierarchies is **best-effort** — the
compiler can warn about a few obvious patterns (omitting `null`,
omitting a known case in some configurations) but it does not in
general prove that the listed patterns cover the closed hierarchy.
The default arm is required boilerplate, and once you have it the
warning goes away.

## F# — non-exhaustive match warning

```fsharp
match decision with
| RowDecision.Accepted t -> /* ... */
| RowDecision.AcceptedWithWarnings (t, ws) -> /* ... */
| RowDecision.Quarantined q -> /* ... */
| RowDecision.SkippedDuplicate s -> /* ... */
| RowDecision.Rejected r -> /* ... */
| RowDecision.Fatal f -> /* ... */
```

Add `| Throttled of ThrottledRow` to the union. **Every** match
expression in the codebase that pattern-matched on `RowDecision`
lights up with **warning FS0025**:

```
warning FS0025: Incomplete pattern matches on this expression.
For example, the value 'Throttled (_)' may indicate a case not covered by the pattern(s).
```

This is *configurable*. By default F# treats FS0025 as a warning.
With `<WarningsAsErrors>FS0025</WarningsAsErrors>` (or `--warnaserror`
in the build), it becomes a compile error. The Fieldale F# engine
ships with that promotion on, so the build *refuses* to compile until
every match is fixed.

The warning lists the missing constructor by name. The fix is
trivial. The bug never reaches a test, let alone production.

## Haskell — `-Wall -Werror`

```haskell
case decision of
  Accepted t                 -> /* ... */
  AcceptedWithWarnings t ws  -> /* ... */
  Quarantined t rs           -> /* ... */
  SkippedDuplicate s         -> /* ... */
  Rejected r                 -> /* ... */
  Fatal f                    -> /* ... */
```

GHC's `-Wincomplete-patterns` warns on the missing constructor. The
project's `cabal.project` sets `-Wall -Werror`, which promotes every
warning to an error.

```
src/FuelUpload/DecisionEngine.hs:42:3: error: [-Wincomplete-patterns, -Werror=incomplete-patterns]
    Pattern match(es) are non-exhaustive
    In a case alternative: Patterns of type 'RowDecision' not matched: Throttled _
```

The build fails. Every place the missing case affects fails at the
same time. No production exposure.

Haskell goes a step further than F# here: there's no concept of "the
default arm" in idiomatic Haskell. You don't write `_ -> error
"unreachable"`. The intended idiom is to **list every constructor**,
and the compiler's job is to confirm the list is complete.

## Rust — compile error, no opt-out

```rust
match decision {
    RowDecision::Accepted(t)               => /* ... */,
    RowDecision::Warning { transaction, warnings } => /* ... */,
    RowDecision::Quarantined { .. }        => /* ... */,
    RowDecision::SkippedDuplicate(s)       => /* ... */,
    RowDecision::Rejected(r)               => /* ... */,
    RowDecision::Fatal(f)                  => /* ... */,
}
```

Add `Throttled(ThrottledRow)` to the enum. Rust's compiler:

```
error[E0004]: non-exhaustive patterns: `RowDecision::Throttled(_)` not covered
  --> src/engine/classifier.rs:42:11
   |
42 |     match decision {
   |           ^^^^^^^^ pattern `RowDecision::Throttled(_)` not covered
```

This is **not a warning that you opted into**. It is the language's
type-checker refusing to compile a `match` that does not cover the
domain. There is no `--allow-non-exhaustive-match` flag. The only way
past this error is to add the arm.

`cargo test` doesn't even reach the test phase. The build fails. The
PR cannot be merged.

## The ladder

| Engine | Detection of new variant | Default arm? | Can be opted out? | Production exposure if forgotten |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| normal C# | none (strings) | implicit no-op | n/a | full — silent miscount |
| idiomatic C# | runtime, on first hit | required `_ => throw` | n/a | partial — loud crash on first new row |
| F# | compile warning FS0025 | optional `_ -> ...` | yes (default is warning) | none if `--warnaserror` is on |
| Haskell | compile error with `-Wall -Werror` | not idiomatic | yes (`-Wno-incomplete-patterns`) | none with project defaults |
| Rust | compile error E0004 | not allowed | **no** | none |

## What "exhaustive" buys you

The reason exhaustiveness matters is not that you'll forget to handle
a case **today**. You won't — when you add `Throttled`, you're aware
of it. The reason it matters is that **the next person to touch the
domain three years from now** also won't forget, because they can't.
The compiler will not let them.

In a domain like fuel upload — where the decision type is the
audit trail, and where missing a case means misposted money — that
property is worth measurable engineering hours per year. It's also
the single hardest property to retrofit. Once you've adopted "the
default arm is fine", the discipline that prevents footgun #7 is no
longer mechanical; it depends on every engineer remembering, every
time, to check every consumer of every sum type.

The compiler is cheaper.

For the narrative version, see [chapter 5 — F# native
features](../part1/05-fsharp-native.qmd) and the journey's
"how easy is it to extend?" question.
